revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Katori jingu]]<ref name=":7">Chiba prefectural government: [http://www.chiba-tour.jp/eng/route/sawara.html Chiba, Katori Shrine]{{Dead link|date=May 2019|bot=InternetArchiveBot|fix-attempted=yes}}</ref> | [[Katori, Chiba]] |Myojin Taisha, [[Chokusaisha]] | ''ichinomiya'' of [[Shimōsa Province]]<ref name="kokugakuin1" /> directly under [[Ise Grand Shrine]], [[Futsunushi]] |- |[[Kashima Jingū]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Kashima, Ibaraki]] |Myojin Taisha, [[Chokusaisha]] | ''ichinomiya'' of [[Hitachi Province]]<ref name="kokugakuin1" /> directly under [[Ise Grand Shrine]], [[Takemikazuchi]] |- |[[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]<ref name="imperial124" /> | [[Yawata]] |[[Kokushi genzaisha]], [[Chokusaisha]] | one of the [[Twenty-Two Shrines]], Upper Seven; ====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ====Imperial shrines, 1st rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.comment: Text replacement - "rank" to "Rank"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Text replacement - "Ichikishimahime" to "Ichikishima-hime"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Ame no Koyane]] → [[Ame-no-Koyane]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Hokkaidō Jingū]] → [[Hokkaidō Jingu]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Izanagi Shrine]] → [[Izanagi Jingu]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kagoshima Shrine]] → [[Kagoshima Jingu]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kashihara Jingu]] → [[Kashihara Shrine]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kashima Jingū]] → [[Kashima Jingu]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kotoshironushi]] → [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Sukunahikona]] → [[Sukunabikona]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Takiribime]] → [[Tagorihime]]
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin ====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[China]]" to "[[:en:China|China]]"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata,
