revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Text replacement - "イチキシマヒメ}}" to "イチキシマヒメ|1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}} |- |[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]<ref name="imperial124" /> | [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] |Myojin Taisha | one of the [[Twenty-Two revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' |{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|タギツヒメ}}, {{ill|Tagorihime|lt=Tagorihime|lt=Takiribime|ja|タキリビメ}} |- |[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]<ref name="imperial124" /> | [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] |Myojin Taisha | one of the [[Twenty-Two Shrines]], Middle Seven; ''ichinomiya'' of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]<ref name="kokugakuin1" /> [[Ōmononushi]] [[:en:Ōnamuchi|Ōnamuchi]] [[:en:Sukunahikona-no-kami|Sukunahikona-no-kami]] revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myojin Taisha'' |{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|イチキシマヒメ|1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|タギツヒメ}}, revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=DHxdSkoo4AMC&pg=PA225 ''Nanʻyō: the rise and fall of the Japanese in Micronesia, 1885-1945,'' pp. 225-229]; n.b., construction completed in 1941</ref> | style="background-color: #ffdddd;" | [[:en:Koror|Koror]], [[:en:Palau|Palau]] | style="background-color: #ffdddd;" | | style="background-color: #ffdddd;" | [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. holy relics and ''kami'' were evacuated by submarine in 1944<ref name=":4">Peattie, [https://books.google.com/books?id=DHxdSkoo4AMC&pg=PA339 p. 339 n61.]</ref> |}

revision.comment: adding the diacritic
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Text replacement - "Kagoshima Jingu" to "Kagoshima Jingū"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Text replacement - "Kirishima Jingu" to "Kirishima Jingū"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: Text replacement - "Kashima Jingu" to "Kashima Jingū"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Text replacement - "Katori Jingu" to "Katori Jingū"
revision.text:
====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, <!-- History offloaded: due to miraheze stability concerns, the edit history of this page has been offloaded to the XML archive. See https://emmaleonhart.github.io/shintowiki-scripts/wikihistory.html?page=Immanuelle%2Fvideos for full history. Last refreshed: [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24796114356|commit triggered pipeline]] --> ====Imperial shrines, 1st Rank==== The Kanpei-taisha were the most highly Ranked shrines in Japan that were officially designated by the government. There were 67 shrines that held this status, which were closely associated with the imperial family. These shrines were considered to be of great historical and cultural significance, and were often visited by members of the imperial family as well as the general public.<ref name="imperial124">[[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124.</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! name ! location !Category ! notes |- |[[Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="imperial125" /> | [[:en:Munakata, Fukuoka|Munakata, Fukuoka]] |''Myōjin Taisha''
